title: What is the Virtual DOM?
excerpt: A deep dive into the lightweight JavaScript representation of the real DOM and why frameworks like React use it.
content: The Virtual DOM is one of those concepts that sounds complicated until you realize it's just a plain JavaScript object that describes what the real DOM should look like. Instead of touching the actual DOM on every state change - which is expensive - you update a cheap in-memory representation first, diff it against the previous one, and only touch the real DOM for what actually changed.
author: Atharv Dange

title: How React's Reconciliation Algorithm Works
excerpt: Understanding the diffing process that makes React updates efficient - keys, fiber, and the 5 cases of reconciliation.
content: Reconciliation is the process React uses to figure out what changed between two renders. The naive approach - compare every node in the tree - would be O(n³). React's heuristic reduces this to O(n) by making two assumptions: elements of different types produce different trees, and developers can hint at stable identity across renders using keys.
author: Atharv Dange

title: CSR vs SSR: It's Not Black and White
excerpt: The rendering strategy debate is more nuanced than Twitter makes it seem. Here's how to actually think about the tradeoffs.
content: Every rendering strategy exists because of a specific tradeoff. CSR gives you a fast server and a dynamic client at the cost of initial load time and SEO. SSR fixes those costs but puts pressure on your server. SSG pre-computes everything for maximum speed but loses freshness. ISR is an attempt to have both freshness and speed, at the cost of complexity. None of these is universally better.
author: Atharv Dange

title: JavaScript Promises: A Deep Dive
excerpt: From callback hell to async/await - understanding promises from the ground up with real analogies.
content: A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. The mental model that clicks for most people: a Promise is like a receipt you get at a restaurant. You don't have your food yet, but you have a guarantee that either you'll get it, or they'll tell you something went wrong.
author: Atharv Dange

title: Building an OIDC Provider from Scratch
excerpt: What I learned from implementing a full spec-compliant OpenID Connect Identity Provider in Express + TypeScript.
content: Most developers treat auth as a black box - you plug in Auth0 or Clerk and move on. I spent months building Torii, a custom OIDC IdP, specifically to understand what's inside the box. The spec is dense but consistent, and once you understand the core flows - authorization code, token exchange, introspection - everything else falls into place.
author: Atharv Dange
